# virtual-header
#	/NOBACKUP/atcca01/zero/ast_v540/sid/ins version 20241230
#	vepfile=/NOBACKUP/atcca01/zero/ast_v540/vep/ADP-AE350-AX45MPV-1C.vep
#	target=sid
#	database /NOBACKUP/atcca01/zero/ast_v540/sid/ins/vep2conf.db
#	mode=alone
#	syscall=elf
#	endian=little
#	run=hello.adx
#	debug_mode=run
#	engine-type=scache
#	format=1
#	architecture=v5
#	multiprocessing=smp
#	sidvep=None
#	custom_attr=None

# virtual-load
load libaudio.la audio_component_library
load libcgencpu.la cgen_component_library
load libconsoles.la console_component_library
load libdmac.la dmac_component_library
load libdummy.la dummy_component_library
load libgloss.la gloss_component_library
load libglue.la glue_component_library
load libgpio.la gpio_component_library
load libinterrupt.la interrupt_component_library
load libloader.la loader_component_library
load libmapper.la mapper_component_library
load libmemory.la mem_component_library
load libndsethc.la NdsEthc_component_library
load libndslcdc.la NdsLcdc_component_library
load librtc.la rtc_component_library
load libsched.la sched_component_library
load libsdc.la sdc_component_library
load libtimers.la timer_component_library
load libuart.la uart_component_library

# virtual-new
new AD1881A_CODEC AC97CODEC
new hw-ahbc-atc020 AHB
new hw-cpu-riscvhf cpu
new hw-dmac-atc300 DMAC
new hw-ethc-atc010 ETHC
new hw-ftsdc010 SDC
new hw-ftsspc010 SSP
new hw-glue-sequence net-init
new hw-glue-sequence net-fini
new hw-glue-sequence net-flush
new hw-glue-sequence net-yield
new hw-glue-sequence net-reset
new hw-gpio-atcgpio100 GPIO
new hw-interrupt-nds-plic PLIC
new hw-interrupt-nds-plic PLIC-SWINT
new hw-lcdc-atc010 LCDC
new hw-memory-ram/rom-basic DRAM
new hw-memory-ram/rom-basic PLDM
new hw-memory-ram/rom-basic SPI_FLASH
new hw-memory-ram/rom-basic SMC_FLASH
new hw-memory-ram/rom-basic HVM
new hw-rtc-atc100 RTC
new hw-timer-atcpit100 Timer
new hw-timer-v5plst V5Timer
new hw-uart-atcuart100 UART1
new hw-uart-atcuart100 UART2
new hw-wdt-atcwdt200 WDT
new nds-dummy SMU
new nds-dummy SPI1
new nds-dummy SPI2
new nds-dummy I2C
new nds-dummy APBBRG
new nds-dummy BMC
new nds-dummy BMC2
new nds-dummy MMCM_DFS
new nds-dummy AHB_DEC
new nds-dummy USB
new nds-dummy L2C
new nds-dummy SID_DUMP
new nds-dummy SMC_REG
new sid-io-stdio stdio
new sid-sched-host-accurate host-sched
new sid-sched-sim target-sched
new sw-gloss-riscv/libgloss gloss
new sw-load-elf-riscv cpu-loader

# net-init
set net-init version-check "1.0.0.0"
set net-init num-outputs "2"
connect-pin main starting -> net-init input

connect-pin net-init output-0 -> net-reset input
connect-pin net-init output-1 -> gloss reset

# net-fini
set net-fini version-check "1.0.0.0"
set net-fini num-outputs "0"
connect-pin main stopping -> net-fini input


# net-flush
set net-flush version-check "1.0.0.0"
set net-flush num-outputs "1"

connect-pin net-flush output-0 -> cpu flush-icache

# net-yield
set net-yield version-check "1.0.0.0"
set net-yield num-outputs "2"

connect-pin net-yield output-0 -> cpu yield
connect-pin net-yield output-1 -> host-sched yield

# net-reset
set net-reset version-check "1.0.0.0"
set net-reset num-outputs "14"

connect-pin net-reset output-0 -> AC97CODEC rst
connect-pin net-reset output-1 -> DMAC HRESET
connect-pin net-reset output-2 -> GPIO reset
connect-pin net-reset output-3 -> LCDC reset!
connect-pin net-reset output-4 -> SDC PRSTn
connect-pin net-reset output-5 -> SSP PRESETn
connect-pin net-reset output-6 -> Timer reset
connect-pin net-reset output-7 -> UART1 Reset
connect-pin net-reset output-8 -> UART2 Reset
connect-pin net-reset output-9 -> V5Timer PRESETn
connect-pin net-reset output-10 -> WDT reset
connect-pin net-reset output-11 -> cpu reset!
connect-pin net-reset output-12 -> cpu-loader load!
connect-pin net-reset output-13 -> AHB reset

# host-sched
set host-sched version-check "1.0.0.0"
set host-sched num-clients "6"
connect-pin main perform-activity -> host-sched advance

set host-sched 0-scale 100
connect-pin host-sched 0-event   -> ETHC aptc-rx-sche-event
connect-pin host-sched 0-control <- ETHC aptc-rx-sche-control

set host-sched 1-scale 100
connect-pin host-sched 1-event   -> ETHC aptc-tx-sche-event
connect-pin host-sched 1-control <- ETHC aptc-tx-sche-control

set host-sched 2-scale 100
connect-pin host-sched 2-event   -> LCDC refresh-sync-event
connect-pin host-sched 2-control <- LCDC refresh-sync-control

set host-sched 3-scale 1000
connect-pin host-sched 3-event   -> RTC rtc-clk-event
connect-pin host-sched 3-control <- RTC rtc-clk-control

set host-sched 4-scale 1
connect-pin host-sched 4-event   -> Timer extclk-event
connect-pin host-sched 4-control <- Timer extclk-control

set host-sched 5-scale 500
set host-sched 5-regular? 1
set host-sched 5-time 1
connect-pin host-sched 5-event   -> stdio poll

# target-sched
set target-sched version-check "1.0.0.0"
set target-sched num-clients "7"
connect-pin main perform-activity -> target-sched advance

set target-sched 0-scale 2
connect-pin target-sched 0-event   -> Timer pclk-event
connect-pin target-sched 0-control <- Timer pclk-control

set target-sched 1-scale 2
connect-pin target-sched 1-event   -> UART1 rx-timeout-event
connect-pin target-sched 1-control <- UART1 rx-timeout-control

set target-sched 2-scale 2
connect-pin target-sched 2-event   -> UART1 tx-timeout-event
connect-pin target-sched 2-control <- UART1 tx-timeout-control

set target-sched 3-scale 2
connect-pin target-sched 3-event   -> UART2 rx-timeout-event
connect-pin target-sched 3-control <- UART2 rx-timeout-control

set target-sched 4-scale 2
connect-pin target-sched 4-event   -> UART2 tx-timeout-event
connect-pin target-sched 4-control <- UART2 tx-timeout-control

set target-sched 5-scale 2
connect-pin target-sched 5-event   -> V5Timer PCLK-clock-event
connect-pin target-sched 5-control <- V5Timer PCLK-clock-control

set target-sched 6-scale 1
connect-pin target-sched 6-event   -> cpu step!
connect-pin target-sched 6-control <- cpu step-cycles

# main
set main persistent? "0"

# gloss
set gloss version-check "1.0.0.0"
set gloss verbose? "0"

#set gloss command-line "executable arg1 arg2 ..."
relate gloss cpu cpu
connect-pin gloss trap <-> cpu trap
connect-pin gloss trap-code <- cpu trap-code
connect-pin gloss process-signal -> main stop!
connect-pin gloss process-signal -> net-yield input

connect-pin gloss debug-tx -> stdio stdout
connect-pin gloss debug-rx <- stdio stdin

connect-bus gloss target-memory AHB access-port

# stdio
set stdio version-check "1.0.0.0"

connect-pin UART1 Sout -> stdio stdout
connect-pin UART1 Sin <- stdio stdin

connect-pin UART2 Sout -> stdio stdout
connect-pin UART2 Sin <- stdio stdin

# cpu-loader
set cpu-loader version-check "1.0.0.0"

set cpu-loader file "hello.adx"
set cpu-loader elf-core-checking 1
relate cpu-loader gloss gloss
relate cpu-loader cpu cpu
connect-bus cpu-loader load-accessor-data AHB access-port
connect-bus cpu-loader load-accessor-insn AHB access-port
connect-pin cpu-loader start-pc-set -> cpu start-pc-set!
connect-pin cpu-loader endian-set -> cpu endian-set!
connect-pin cpu-loader error -> main stop!

# AC97CODEC
set AC97CODEC version-check "1.0.0.0"
connect-pin SSP txd_r -> AC97CODEC SDATA_OUT
connect-pin SSP ac97_resetn_r -> AC97CODEC RESET

# AHB
connect-bus AHB region=0[0x00000000-0x07ffffff] DRAM read-write-port
connect-bus AHB region=1[0x80000000-0x801fffff] SPI_FLASH read-write-port
connect-bus AHB region=2[0x88000000-0x881fffff] SMC_FLASH read-write-port
connect-bus AHB region=3[0x90000000-0x9fffffff] HVM read-write-port
connect-bus AHB region=4[0xa0000000-0xa03fffff] cpu access-port
connect-bus AHB region=5[0xe0100000-0xe01fffff] ETHC registers
connect-bus AHB region=6[0xe0200000-0xe02fffff] LCDC registers
connect-bus AHB region=7[0xe6800000-0xe68fffff] PLDM read-write-port
connect-bus AHB region=8[0xf0c00000-0xf0cfffff] DMAC registers

# AHB_DEC
set AHB_DEC version-check "1.0.0.0"

# APB
connect-bus AHB region=9[0xc0000000-0xc00fffff] BMC registers
connect-bus AHB region=10[0xc0100000-0xc01fffff] BMC2 registers
connect-bus AHB region=11[0xc0200000-0xc02fffff] MMCM_DFS registers
connect-bus AHB region=12[0xe0000000-0xe00fffff] AHB_DEC registers
connect-bus AHB region=13[0xe0300000-0xe03fffff] USB registers
connect-bus AHB region=14[0xe0400000-0xe04fffff] SMC_REG registers
connect-bus AHB region=15[0xe0500000-0xe05fffff] L2C registers
connect-bus AHB region=16[0xe4000000-0xe43fffff] PLIC registers
connect-bus AHB region=17[0xe6000000-0xe60fffff] V5Timer registers
connect-bus AHB region=18[0xe6400000-0xe67fffff] PLIC-SWINT registers
connect-bus AHB region=19[0xf0000000-0xf00fffff] APBBRG registers
connect-bus AHB region=20[0xf0100000-0xf01fffff] SMU registers
connect-bus AHB region=21[0xf0200000-0xf02fffff] UART1 registers
connect-bus AHB region=22[0xf0300000-0xf03fffff] UART2 registers
connect-bus AHB region=23[0xf0400000-0xf04fffff] Timer registers
connect-bus AHB region=24[0xf0500000-0xf05fffff] WDT registers
connect-bus AHB region=25[0xf0600000-0xf06fffff] RTC registers
connect-bus AHB region=26[0xf0700000-0xf07fffff] GPIO registers
connect-bus AHB region=27[0xf0800000-0xf08fffff] SID_DUMP registers
connect-bus AHB region=28[0xf0a00000-0xf0afffff] I2C registers
connect-bus AHB region=29[0xf0b00000-0xf0bfffff] SPI1 registers
connect-bus AHB region=30[0xf0d00000-0xf0dfffff] SSP read-write-port
connect-bus AHB region=31[0xf0e00000-0xf0efffff] SDC read-write-port
connect-bus AHB region=32[0xf0f00000-0xf0ffffff] SPI2 registers

# APBBRG
set APBBRG version-check "1.0.0.0"

# BMC
set BMC version-check "1.0.0.0"

# BMC2
set BMC2 version-check "1.0.0.0"

# cpu
set cpu version-check "1.0.0.1"
set cpu endian "little"
set cpu engine-type "scache"
set cpu insn-count "0"
set cpu cpu-option "--conf-cpu-model ax45mpv --conf-isa-32 off --init-mhartid 0 --conf-pipeline on --init-nba 0x80 --conf-isa-c on --conf-isa-a on --conf-isa-v on --conf-veccfg on --conf-isa-zvlsseg on --conf-mvec-cfg-minor 0 --conf-mvec-cfg-major 1 --conf-msc-ext3 on --conf-isa-vl4 on --conf-isa-vsih on --conf-isa-vdot on --conf-isa-vpfh on --conf-isa-zvqmac on --conf-disable-vector-load-store-vstart-support on --conf-mvec-cfg-misew 2 --conf-mvec-cfg-mfsew 2 --conf-mmsc-cfg-hvmcsr on --conf-hvm-size 512KB --init-hvm-base 0x90000000 --conf-mhvm-cfg-bank on --conf-mhvm-cfg-bsel 0x12 --conf-mhvm-cfg-sub on --conf-crashsave off --conf-mul fast --conf-isa-d on --conf-isa-f on --conf-isa-efhw off --conf-isa-zfh on --conf-isa-bf16cvt on --conf-isa-zfbfmin on --conf-isa-zvfbfmin on --conf-isa-zvfbfwma on --conf-isa-codense on --conf-ras on --conf-btb on --conf-pfm on --conf-pfm-nds on --conf-powerbrake on --conf-ilm off --conf-ilm-xonly off --conf-dlm off --conf-icache on --conf-icache-line-size 64B --conf-icache-set 256 --conf-icache-way 2 --conf-icache-lock on --conf-micm-cfg-ic-repl pseudo_lru --conf-icache-ecc null --conf-dcache on --conf-dcache-line-size 64B --conf-dcache-set 128 --conf-dcache-way 4 --conf-dcache-lock on --conf-mdcm-cfg-dc-repl pseudo_lru --conf-dcache-ecc null --conf-dcache-warnd on --conf-nbld-en on --conf-nbld-fp-en on --conf-cctl on --conf-cctl-version 1 --conf-mmu off --conf-pmp 8 --conf-pmp-granularity 4096 --conf-pmp-na4 off --conf-pma 8 --conf-user-mode on --conf-supervisor-mode on --conf-va-width 32 --conf-pa-width 32 --conf-bus-width 512 --conf-stack-protection on --conf-unaligned-access on --conf-vectored-plic on --conf-reset-gpr off --conf-csr-property 0x7ca:0x1800:RO:0x3 --conf-mmsc-cfg-rvarch on --conf-bus-overlap on --conf-isa-v5 on --conf-profile-fastl1 on --ideal-zero-memory-latency off --conf-memory-delay 30 --conf-isa-dsp off --conf-isa-zba on --conf-isa-zbb on --conf-isa-zbc on --conf-isa-zbs on --conf-env-mode user --conf-exception-abort on --init-icache-en on --init-dcache-en on"
set cpu step-insn-count "10000"
set cpu enable-step-trap? "false"
set cpu trace-filename "-"
set cpu trace-extract? "false"
set cpu trace-semantics? "false"
set cpu trace-disassemble? "false"
set cpu trace-result? "false"
set cpu trace-counter? "false"
set cpu final-insn-count? "false"
set cpu step-cycles ""
set cpu trap ""
set cpu trap-code ""
set cpu MHZ "60"
set cpu maximum_num_of_intrpt "64"
set cpu device-region "[0xe6800000-0xe68fffff],[0xe0100000-0xe01fffff],[0x80000000-0x801fffff],[0xf0200000-0xf02fffff],[0xf0300000-0xf03fffff],[0xf0400000-0xf04fffff],[0xf0500000-0xf05fffff],[0xf0600000-0xf06fffff],[0xf0700000-0xf07fffff],[0xf0e00000-0xf0efffff],[0xf0d00000-0xf0dfffff],[0xf0100000-0xf01fffff],[0xe6000000-0xe60fffff],[0xe4000000-0xe5ffffff],[0xe6400000-0xe67fffff],[0xe0000000-0xe00fffff],[0xf0b00000-0xf0bfffff],[0xf0f00000-0xf0ffffff],[0xf0000000-0xf00fffff],[0xf0a00000-0xf0afffff],[0x88000000-0x881fffff],[0xc0000000-0xc00fffff],[0xe0400000-0xe04fffff],[0xe0200000-0xe02fffff],[0xf0c00000-0xf0cfffff]"
set cpu hvm-region "[0x90000000-0x9fffffff]"
connect-pin PLIC interrupt-target-0 -> cpu meip
connect-pin PLIC-SWINT interrupt-target-0 -> cpu msip
connect-pin V5Timer intr_pin -> cpu mtip
connect-pin PLIC interrupt-target-0-source-id -> cpu intr_id
connect-pin cpu time-query -> target-sched time-query
connect-pin cpu time-high  <- target-sched time-high
connect-pin cpu time-low   <- target-sched time-low
connect-bus cpu data-memory AHB access-port
connect-bus cpu insn-memory AHB access-port

# DMAC
set DMAC version-check "1.0.0.0"
set DMAC endianess "2"

# DRAM
set DRAM version-check "1.0.0.0"
set DRAM size "0x08000000"

# ETHC
set ETHC version-check "1.0.0.0"
set ETHC bus-endian "1"
set ETHC host-endian "2"
connect-bus ETHC to-access-port AHB access-port

# GPIO
set GPIO version-check "1.0.0.0"
set GPIO id-revision "0x02031000"
set GPIO configuration "0xe0000020"
set GPIO major-revision "0x00"
set GPIO minor-revision "0x0"
set GPIO number-of-channels "0x20"
set GPIO pclk-scale "1.0"
set GPIO extclk-scale "1.0"
connect-pin GPIO time-query -> target-sched time-query
connect-pin GPIO time-high  <- target-sched time-high
connect-pin GPIO time-low   <- target-sched time-low

# HVM
set HVM version-check "1.0.0.0"
set HVM size "0x10000000"

# I2C
set I2C version-check "1.0.0.0"

# L2C
set L2C version-check "1.0.0.0"

# LCDC
set LCDC version-check "1.0.0.0"
set LCDC bus-endian "1"
set LCDC absolute-sync "1"
connect-pin LCDC time-query -> target-sched time-query
connect-pin LCDC time-high  <- target-sched time-high
connect-pin LCDC time-low   <- target-sched time-low
connect-bus LCDC frame-buffer AHB access-port

# MMCM_DFS
set MMCM_DFS version-check "1.0.0.0"

# PLDM
set PLDM version-check "1.0.0.0"
set PLDM size "0x00100000"

# PLIC
set PLIC version-check "1.0.0.0"
connect-pin UART1 INTR -> PLIC interrupt-source-8
connect-pin UART2 INTR -> PLIC interrupt-source-9
connect-pin Timer interrupt -> PLIC interrupt-source-3
connect-pin GPIO interrupt -> PLIC interrupt-source-7
connect-pin ETHC irq-out -> PLIC interrupt-source-19
connect-pin SDC sdc_intr -> PLIC interrupt-source-18
connect-pin RTC rtc-int-alarm -> PLIC interrupt-source-2
connect-pin RTC rtc-int-sec -> PLIC interrupt-source-1
connect-pin cpu ack_plic -> PLIC interrupt-target-0-intr-handle-ack
connect-pin SSP ssp_intr -> PLIC interrupt-source-17
connect-pin DMAC dmaint -> PLIC interrupt-source-10

# PLIC-SWINT
set PLIC-SWINT version-check "1.0.0.0"

# RTC
set RTC version-check "1.0.0.0"

# SDC
set SDC version-check "1.0.0.0"
set SDC fifo-depth "4"

# SID_DUMP
set SID_DUMP version-check "1.0.0.0"

# SMC_FLASH
set SMC_FLASH version-check "1.0.0.0"
set SMC_FLASH size "0x00200000"

# SMC_REG
set SMC_REG version-check "1.0.0.0"

# SMU
set SMU version-check "1.0.0.0"

# SPI1
set SPI1 version-check "1.0.0.0"

# SPI2
set SPI2 version-check "1.0.0.0"

# SPI_FLASH
set SPI_FLASH version-check "1.0.0.0"
set SPI_FLASH size "0x00200000"

# SSP
set SSP version-check "1.0.0.0"
set SSP tx-fifo-depth "8"
set SSP rx-fifo-depth "8"
connect-pin AC97CODEC SDATA_IN -> SSP rxd

# Timer
set Timer version-check "1.0.0.0"
set Timer id-revision "0x03031000"
set Timer configuration "0x4"
set Timer major-revision "0x00"
set Timer minor-revision "0x0"
set Timer number-of-channels "0x4"
set Timer max-tick-step "0x1000"
set Timer pclk-scale "1.0"

connect-pin Timer time-query -> target-sched time-query
connect-pin Timer time-high  <- target-sched time-high
connect-pin Timer time-low   <- target-sched time-low

# UART1
set UART1 version-check "1.0.0.0"
set UART1 id-revision "0x02010000"
set UART1 configuration "0x0"
set UART1 major-revision "0x000"
set UART1 minor-revision "0x0"
set UART1 fifo-depth "0x10"

# UART2
set UART2 version-check "1.0.0.0"
set UART2 id-revision "0x02010000"
set UART2 configuration "0x0"
set UART2 major-revision "0x000"
set UART2 minor-revision "0x0"
set UART2 fifo-depth "0x10"

# USB
set USB version-check "1.0.0.0"

# V5Timer
set V5Timer version-check "1.0.0.0"
set V5Timer MHZ "60"
set V5Timer enhance? "0"
relate V5Timer cpu cpu

# WDT
set WDT version-check "1.0.0.0"
set WDT id-revision "0x03002000"
set WDT major-revision "0x00"
set WDT minor-revision "0x0"
set WDT pclk-scale "1.0"
set WDT extclk-scale "1.0"
set WDT atcwdt200-wp-num "0x5aa5"
set WDT atcwdt200-restart-num "0xcafe"
connect-pin WDT time-query -> target-sched time-query
connect-pin WDT time-high  <- target-sched time-high
connect-pin WDT time-low   <- target-sched time-low

input.prompt:
I'm designing an autonomous agentic optimization workflow for libnn (Andes NN Library for RISC-V). The workflow will be driven by Claude Code as the optimization agent.

**Context:**
- libnn has ~411 C source files with multi-variant implementations (plain C / DSP / RVV vector)
- Each function has preprocessor guards: `#ifdef ENA_VEC_ISA` for vector, `#ifdef ENA_DSP_ISA` for DSP
- Build system: `build.sh <CPU>` produces `libnn.a`
- Correctness: QEMU tests via `qemu-validate/run_qemu_test.sh <func> --strace --vlen 1024`
- Performance: FPGA cycle counts via `test_perf.sh ax45mpv riscv64-elf-gcc BOARD <func>`
- FPGA config files (`.cfg`) define hardware features: VLEN, ELEN, ISA extensions (zba, zbb, zfh, zvfh, vdot, etc.)

**Two modes:**
1. Single function: optimize one libnn function with specified input shapes
2. Model: optimize a set of libnn function calls (with shapes) that comprise an AI model

**Configuration needs:**
- Toolchain path, prefix, compiler
- Target CPU / FPGA config file path (to auto-extract features)
- QEMU binary path
- FPGA address (host:port)

**Constraints:**
- Each implementation step must have exactly ONE feature and verifiable test cases
- Python for orchestration/parsing, shell for build/test/perf (reuse existing scripts)
- Must work both as Claude Code interactive tools AND as automated pipeline

**Questions to answer:**
1. What's the right directory/module structure?
2. What's the right config file format (YAML vs TOML)?
3. How should the "agent loop" work - should we define a formal state machine or keep it simple?
4. How should input shapes be specified - tied to existing test cases or custom?
5. What's the minimal dependency set?
6. Should the workflow support "dry run" mode for testing without FPGA?

Please provide a detailed architectural design with file structure, interfaces between components, data flow, and key design decisions. Focus on practical implementation, not aspirational features.

content.text:
Now I have all the information needed. Let me formalize the design.

---

## Architectural Design: Agentic libnn Optimization Workflow

### 1. Design Decisions (Answering Your Six Questions)

**Q1: Directory/module structure**

Place a new `opt/` directory at the repository root. This keeps the optimization tooling co-located with the library without polluting `Source/`, `qemu-validate/`, or `scripts/`. The `opt/` directory is self-contained: it has its own Python package, shell wrappers, and a TOML configuration file.

```
agentic_libnn_opt/
  opt/                          # NEW: optimization workflow root
    __init__.py                 # makes it a package (empty)
    config.py                   # Pydantic models for config + target features
    config.toml                 # user-editable config (paths, target, FPGA)
    runner.py                   # shell command runner (build, qemu, fpga)
    parser.py                   # parse QEMU/FPGA output, objdump, .cfg files
    state.py                    # optimization state machine + journal
    agent.py                    # agent loop entry point (single-func + model modes)
    shapes.py                   # input shape specifications + model definitions
    cli.py                      # click CLI: `python -m opt run`, `python -m opt status`
    __main__.py                 # `python -m opt` entry point -> cli.py
    
    shell/                      # thin shell wrappers (called by runner.py)
      build.sh                  # wraps ../../build.sh with toolchain PATH setup
      qemu_test.sh              # wraps ../../qemu-validate/run_qemu_test.sh
      fpga_test.sh              # wraps ../../qemu-validate/run_fpga_test.sh
      rebuild_single.sh         # wraps ../../qemu-validate/rebuild_single.sh

    models/                     # model definition files (TOML)
      example_yolov8n_s8.toml   # example: YOLOv8-nano int8 operator list

  .optdb/                       # gitignored: optimization run state
    journal.jsonl               # append-only log of every action+result
    current_run.json            # current optimization state snapshot
    baselines/                  # captured baseline perf numbers
      <func_name>.json
```

**Rationale**: The `opt/` package is importable by Claude Code tools directly. Shell wrappers in `opt/shell/` are thin (5-10 lines) -- they set up PATH and delegate to the existing scripts. The `.optdb/` directory is gitignored for ephemeral state; the `journal.jsonl` provides a crash-recoverable audit trail.

**Q2: Config file format -- TOML**

TOML, not YAML. Reasons:

1. TOML has typed values natively (integers, strings, booleans, arrays) with no implicit coercion. YAML's `on`/`off` -> boolean footgun is particularly dangerous here since the QEMU cfg files use `"on"`/`"off"` strings for ISA features.
2. Python 3.11+ has `tomllib` in stdlib. You already have `tomlkit` (0.13.2) which gives round-trip editing.
3. TOML's `[section]` syntax maps cleanly to the hierarchical config (toolchain, target, qemu, fpga).
4. The QEMU `.cfg` files already use INI-like `[section]` syntax with `property = "value"`, making TOML feel natural for users who edit both.

**Q3: Agent loop -- lightweight state machine, NOT a formal FSM library**

Use a simple loop with an explicit `Phase` enum and a journal. The phases are:

```
ANALYZE -> PLAN -> IMPLEMENT -> BUILD -> VALIDATE_QEMU -> VALIDATE_FPGA -> MEASURE -> EVALUATE -> (loop or DONE)
```

This is NOT a full FSM with transition tables. It is a while loop with a phase variable and a `match` statement. The journal captures every transition. If Claude Code is interrupted, it reads the journal on restart and resumes at the last incomplete phase.

**Rationale**: A formal FSM library (e.g., `transitions`) adds a dependency and abstraction that is harder for Claude Code to reason about. The explicit loop with phases is readable, debuggable, and trivially serializable. Claude Code's agent can also skip phases (e.g., skip `VALIDATE_FPGA` in dry-run mode).

**Q4: Input shapes -- dual approach**

- **Default**: Use the existing test cases in `Examples/unit_func/t_*.c`. The shapes are hardcoded in the `main()` functions. The parser extracts them by reading the C source and parsing the variable assignments in the `#if !ENA_MEASURE_PERF` and `ENA_MEASURE_PERF` blocks. The perf-mode test (test-3 in the conv example) is always present and uses the largest/most representative shape.
- **Custom shapes**: For model mode, shapes are specified in a TOML model definition file. These shapes are injected by generating a custom test harness (a temporary `.c` file) or by using the existing test with different golden data paths. Given the existing test infrastructure relies on pre-computed golden data in `bin/`, the pragmatic approach is: use QEMU in correctness mode with the existing test shapes, but when measuring FPGA cycles for model-relevant shapes, generate a custom test harness that calls the function with the specified shape (without golden-data verification, since the QEMU correctness pass already validates the algorithm).

**Q5: Minimal dependency set**

Python stdlib plus these (all already installed):
- `tomlkit` -- config read/write
- `pydantic` -- config validation and state models
- `click` -- CLI

No new pip installs required. The shell scripts use only `bash`, `expect`, `strace`, and the Andes toolchain binaries.

**Q6: Dry-run mode**

Yes. Dry-run mode means:
- Build: runs normally (uses local toolchain)
- QEMU validation: runs normally (QEMU is local)
- FPGA measurement: **skipped** -- the agent reports "FPGA dry-run: skipped" and uses estimated cycle counts from the objdump instruction count as a rough proxy (or cached baseline values)

This is controlled by a `dry_run = true` flag in `config.toml` and also a `--dry-run` CLI flag.

---

### 2. Component Design

#### 2.1 `config.toml` and `config.py`

The configuration file at `/home/nick/work/agentic_libnn_opt/opt/config.toml`:

```toml
[toolchain]
bin_dir = "/local/nick/SW_Release_cp/ast530/nds64le-elf-newlib-v5d/bin"
prefix = "riscv64-elf-"
compiler = "riscv64-elf-gcc"

[target]
cpu = "AX45MPV"
# SID .conf file -- parsed to extract VLEN, ELEN, ISA extensions
sid_conf = "project/ax45mpv/ADP-AE350-AX45MPV-1C.conf"
# QEMU .cfg file -- parsed to extract QEMU-level features
qemu_cfg = "project/ax45mpv/qemu_cfg/zve64d/ADP-AE350-AX45MPV-1C_dw0_mw1.cfg"

[qemu]
binary = "/local/nick/qemu_andes/build/qemu-system-riscv64"
vlen = 1024
timeout = 120
use_strace = true

[fpga]
host = "sw-boards.andestech.com"
port = 1116
timeout = 300
dry_run = false

[agent]
max_iterations = 10
min_improvement_pct = 2.0  # stop if improvement < 2%
```

`config.py` contains Pydantic models that:
1. Load and validate `config.toml`
2. Parse the QEMU `.cfg` file to auto-extract target features (VLEN, ELEN, zba, zbb, zfh, zvfh, vdot, etc.)
3. Parse the SID `.conf` file to extract `cpu-option` flags (the `--conf-isa-vdot`, `--conf-isa-vpfh`, etc.)
4. Expose a `TargetFeatures` dataclass with all hardware capabilities

The parser for the `.cfg` file is straightforward: it is INI-like with `[global]` sections containing `property`/`value` pairs for the `andes-ax45mpv-riscv-cpu` driver, plus `[andes-config "cpu"]` sections with `key = "value"` pairs.

#### 2.2 `runner.py` -- Command Executor

This module wraps every external command. It is the ONLY module that calls `subprocess`. Every call:
1. Logs the full command to the journal
2. Captures stdout, stderr, return code
3. Returns a `RunResult` dataclass with all three plus wall-clock time

Key methods:
- `build_libnn(cpu: str) -> RunResult` -- calls `build.sh`
- `rebuild_single(source_path: str) -> RunResult` -- calls `rebuild_single.sh` for incremental builds
- `qemu_test(func_name: str, vlen: int) -> RunResult` -- calls `run_qemu_test.sh`
- `fpga_test(func_name: str) -> RunResult` -- calls `run_fpga_test.sh` or `test_perf.sh` single-func mode
- `objdump_function(func_name: str) -> str` -- extracts disassembly for a specific function from `libnn.objdump`

Each method uses `opt/shell/*.sh` wrappers that handle PATH setup and argument translation.

#### 2.3 `parser.py` -- Output Parsers

Parses all structured output from the shell tools:
- `parse_qemu_result(output: str) -> QemuResult` -- extracts PASS/FAIL and sub-test counts from strace output
- `parse_fpga_result(output: str) -> FpgaResult` -- extracts cycle count, instruction count, IPC, pass/fail
- `parse_perf_csv(path: str) -> dict[str, PerfRecord]` -- parses the `performance.csv` from full test suite runs
- `parse_qemu_cfg(path: str) -> TargetFeatures` -- extracts HW features from `.cfg` files
- `parse_sid_conf(path: str) -> dict` -- extracts cpu-option from `.conf` files
- `parse_test_shapes(test_c_path: str) -> list[ShapeSpec]` -- extracts shape parameters from `t_*.c` files via regex on the variable assignments (the `in_tensor_dim_x = 32;` pattern)
- `extract_objdump_function(objdump_path: str, func_name: str) -> str` -- extracts a single function's disassembly

#### 2.4 `state.py` -- Optimization State

```python
class Phase(str, Enum):
    ANALYZE = "analyze"        # read source, extract current impl, get baseline
    PLAN = "plan"              # Claude Code plans the optimization approach
    IMPLEMENT = "implement"    # Claude Code edits the source file
    BUILD = "build"            # compile (incremental or full)
    VALIDATE_QEMU = "validate_qemu"  # correctness via QEMU
    VALIDATE_FPGA = "validate_fpga"  # correctness on FPGA (optional)
    MEASURE = "measure"        # cycle count on FPGA
    EVALUATE = "evaluate"      # compare to baseline, decide next step
    DONE = "done"

class OptimizationState(BaseModel):
    run_id: str
    func_name: str
    source_path: str
    phase: Phase
    iteration: int
    baseline_cycles: int | None
    current_cycles: int | None
    best_cycles: int | None
    best_iteration: int | None
    history: list[IterationRecord]
```

The journal (`journal.jsonl`) is an append-only file where each line is a JSON object:
```json
{"ts": "2026-04-15T10:30:00", "run_id": "abc123", "phase": "build", "action": "rebuild_single", "cmd": "...", "rc": 0, "duration_s": 2.3, "result_summary": "OK"}
```

On crash recovery, `state.py` reads the journal, reconstructs the state, and resumes.

#### 2.5 `shapes.py` -- Shape Specifications

For single-function mode:
```python
@dataclass
class ConvShape:
    in_x: int; in_y: int; in_ch: int; batch: int
    out_ch: int; ker_x: int; ker_y: int
    pad_x: int; pad_y: int; stride_x: int; stride_y: int
```

For model mode, a TOML file like `opt/models/example_yolov8n_s8.toml`:
```toml
name = "YOLOv8n-s8"

[[ops]]
func = "conv_1x1_HWC_s8_s8_s8_asym_bias_fast_any"
shapes = { in_x = 80, in_y = 80, in_ch = 64, batch = 1, out_ch = 64, ker_x = 1, ker_y = 1 }
count = 3  # appears 3 times in the model

[[ops]]
func = "conv_HWC_s8_s8_s8_asym_bias_any"
shapes = { in_x = 40, in_y = 40, in_ch = 128, batch = 1, out_ch = 128, ker_x = 3, ker_y = 3 }
count = 6
```

The `shapes.py` module also provides `extract_shapes_from_test(test_c_path)` that parses existing test C files to pull out the shape parameters. This reuses the `dump_param()` format string as a parsing guide.

#### 2.6 `agent.py` -- The Agent Loop

This is the main entry point for Claude Code. Two modes:

**Single-function mode:**
```
python -m opt run --func conv_HWC_s8_s8_s8_asym_bias_any
```

The agent loop:
```python
def run_optimization(func_name: str, config: Config):
    state = OptimizationState.init(func_name, config)
    
    while state.phase != Phase.DONE and state.iteration < config.agent.max_iterations:
        match state.phase:
            case Phase.ANALYZE:
                # 1. Find source file: Source/*/<func_name>.c
                # 2. Read it, identify current impl variant (plain/DSP/RVV)
                # 3. Get baseline cycles (from cache or FPGA run)
                # 4. Extract objdump for the function
                # 5. Transition -> PLAN
                
            case Phase.PLAN:
                # Claude Code reviews source + objdump + hardware features
                # Produces a plan dict: { "approach": "...", "changes": [...] }
                # Transition -> IMPLEMENT
                
            case Phase.IMPLEMENT:
                # Claude Code edits the source file
                # Exactly ONE logical change per iteration
                # Transition -> BUILD
                
            case Phase.BUILD:
                # Call rebuild_single.sh for the changed file
                # On failure -> revert and go to PLAN with error context
                # On success -> VALIDATE_QEMU
                
            case Phase.VALIDATE_QEMU:
                # Run QEMU test (all VLENs: 1024, 512, 256)
                # On failure -> revert and go to PLAN with error context
                # On success -> MEASURE (or VALIDATE_FPGA if not dry_run)
                
            case Phase.VALIDATE_FPGA:
                # Optional: run on FPGA for correctness
                # Skipped in dry_run mode
                
            case Phase.MEASURE:
                # Run FPGA perf test, get cycle count
                # Skipped in dry_run mode (use objdump inst count estimate)
                
            case Phase.EVALUATE:
                # Compare current_cycles to baseline_cycles
                # If improvement >= threshold -> accept, update best
                # If regression -> revert
                # If max_iterations reached -> DONE
                # Else -> PLAN for next iteration
```

**Model mode:**
```
python -m opt run --model models/yolov8n_s8.toml
```

In model mode, the agent iterates over the operator list sorted by `count * baseline_cycles` (highest total cost first). It runs the single-function optimization loop for each operator, then reports the total model-level improvement.

#### 2.7 `cli.py` -- Click CLI

```
python -m opt run --func <name>          # single function optimization
python -m opt run --model <path.toml>    # model-level optimization
python -m opt run --func <name> --dry-run  # no FPGA
python -m opt status                     # show current optimization state
python -m opt baseline --func <name>     # capture baseline perf numbers
python -m opt baseline --all             # capture all baselines
python -m opt features                   # dump parsed target features
python -m opt journal                    # show journal tail
```

---

### 3. Data Flow

```
User/Claude Code
      |
      v
  cli.py (click)
      |
      v
  agent.py (phase loop)
      |
      +---> config.py (read config.toml, parse .cfg/.conf for features)
      |
      +---> state.py (read/write .optdb/current_run.json + journal.jsonl)
      |
      +---> runner.py (subprocess calls)
      |        |
      |        +---> opt/shell/build.sh -----> ../../build.sh
      |        +---> opt/shell/rebuild_single.sh -> ../../qemu-validate/rebuild_single.sh
      |        +---> opt/shell/qemu_test.sh ----> ../../qemu-validate/run_qemu_test.sh
      |        +---> opt/shell/fpga_test.sh ----> ../../qemu-validate/run_fpga_test.sh
      |        |                                  (or ../../test_perf.sh single-func mode)
      |        v
      |     RunResult (stdout, stderr, rc, duration)
      |
      +---> parser.py (parse RunResult -> structured data)
               |
               v
          QemuResult / FpgaResult / TargetFeatures / ShapeSpec
```

---

### 4. Integration with Claude Code

Claude Code interacts with this system in two ways:

**As an interactive tool user**: Claude Code calls `python -m opt baseline --func <name>` to get baseline numbers, reads source files directly, edits them, then calls `python -m opt run --func <name> --dry-run` to validate.

**As the optimization agent**: The `agent.py` loop is designed so that Claude Code IS the agent. The `PLAN` and `IMPLEMENT` phases are where Claude Code does its work (reading source, analyzing objdump, writing code). The other phases are automated (build, test, measure). The journal gives Claude Code context when resuming.

The key insight is that `agent.py` does NOT need to call Claude Code's API. Instead, Claude Code runs the `agent.py` phases as tools, and the human-in-the-loop (or Claude Code itself in autonomous mode) handles the PLAN and IMPLEMENT phases by reading files and editing code. The agent loop becomes a structured TODO list that Claude Code follows.

For fully autonomous operation, `agent.py` would expose the phase-appropriate context as tool output (e.g., "You are in PLAN phase. Source code: ..., Baseline cycles: ..., Hardware features: ..."), and Claude Code generates the plan + implementation.

---

### 5. Key Implementation Details

**Incremental builds are critical**: A full `build.sh AX45MPV` compiles ~407 files. The `rebuild_single.sh` script compiles 1 file and updates the `.a` archive, making the edit-build-test cycle ~100x faster. The runner should always use incremental builds during optimization and only do a full build at the start (to create `lib_objs/`).

**QEMU validation at multiple VLENs**: The QEMU test should run at VLEN=1024 (primary target) and VLEN=256 (portability check). The QEMU command line already supports `vlen=N` override. Fail at any VLEN = revert.

**FPGA is a shared resource**: The FPGA board at `sw-boards.andestech.com:1116` is a single-threaded resource (GDB connection). The runner must acquire a lock before FPGA operations. A simple file lock (`/tmp/libnn_fpga.lock`) suffices.

**Source file location**: Given function name `conv_HWC_s8_s8_s8_asym_bias_any`, the source file is `Source/ConvolutionFunctions/riscv_nn_conv_HWC_s8_s8_s8_asym_bias_any.c`. The mapping is: `Source/<Category>/riscv_nn_<func_name>.c`. The category can be resolved by globbing `Source/*/riscv_nn_<func_name>.c`.

**Revert mechanism**: Before each IMPLEMENT phase, `runner.py` creates a backup of the source file (e.g., `cp source.c source.c.bak`). On revert, it restores the backup and rebuilds. The git working tree is NOT used for revert (avoids accidental commits).

---

### 6. Implementation Sequence

1. **Phase 1**: `config.toml` + `config.py` + `parser.py` (cfg/conf parsing) -- enables `python -m opt features`
2. **Phase 2**: `runner.py` + `opt/shell/*.sh` wrappers -- enables `python -m opt baseline`
3. **Phase 3**: `state.py` + `agent.py` (single-func mode, QEMU-only dry-run) -- the core loop
4. **Phase 4**: `shapes.py` + model TOML format -- enables model mode
5. **Phase 5**: `cli.py` polish, journal viewer, status dashboard

---

### 7. Potential Challenges

1. **Test shape extraction from C source**: The `t_*.c` files have varied formats. Some use `uint16_t` variables, some use `uint32_t`, some use `int32_t`. The parser needs to handle the `main()` function's variable assignments robustly. Regex on `<var_name> = <value>;` patterns should work for 95% of cases. The remaining 5% can fall back to "use existing test as-is."

2. **FPGA flakiness**: Board connections can drop. The GDB expect script has a 600-second timeout. `runner.py` should retry FPGA operations once on failure, and the journal should log connection errors distinctly from test failures.

3. **Baseline stability**: FPGA cycle counts have some variance (typically <1%). The baseline measurement should run 2-3 times and take the minimum. The `ENA_RUN_TWICE` macro in the existing test infrastructure already helps (the second run is with warm caches).

4. **Multiple implementation variants**: A source file can have up to 3 code paths (`#ifdef ENA_VEC_ISA`, `#ifdef ENA_DSP_ISA`, plain C). The agent needs to know which path is active for the target CPU. The `config.py` module resolves this from the build flags (e.g., AX45MPV always has `ENA_VEC_ISA`).

### Critical Files for Implementation
- `/home/nick/work/agentic_libnn_opt/build.sh` -- must be wrapped, its CPU-to-flags mapping is the source of truth for compilation flags
- `/home/nick/work/agentic_libnn_opt/qemu-validate/run_qemu_test.sh` -- the QEMU validation script to wrap
- `/home/nick/work/agentic_libnn_opt/qemu-validate/rebuild_single.sh` -- the incremental build script to wrap
- `/home/nick/work/agentic_libnn_opt/test_perf.sh` -- the FPGA perf measurement script (single-func mode at line 263-311)
- `/home/nick/work/agentic_libnn_opt/project/ax45mpv/qemu_cfg/zve64d/ADP-AE350-AX45MPV-1C_dw0_mw1.cfg` -- the QEMU config file that the parser needs to extract hardware features from

```
  opt/                          # NEW: optimization workflow root
    __init__.py                 # makes it a package (empty)
    config.py                   # Pydantic models for config + target features
    config.toml                 # user-editable config (paths, target, FPGA)
    runner.py                   # shell command runner (build, qemu, fpga)
    parser.py                   # parse QEMU/FPGA output, objdump, .cfg files
    state.py                    # optimization state machine + journal
    agent.py                    # agent loop entry point (single-func + model modes)
    shapes.py                   # input shape specifications + model definitions
    cli.py                      # click CLI: `python -m opt run`, `python -m opt status`
    __main__.py                 # `python -m opt` entry point -> cli.py
    
    shell/                      # thin shell wrappers (called by runner.py)
      build.sh                  # wraps ../../build.sh with toolchain PATH setup
      qemu_test.sh              # wraps ../../qemu-validate/run_qemu_test.sh
      fpga_test.sh              # wraps ../../qemu-validate/run_fpga_test.sh
      rebuild_single.sh         # wraps ../../qemu-validate/rebuild_single.sh

    models/                     # model definition files (TOML)
      example_yolov8n_s8.toml   # example: YOLOv8-nano int8 operator list

  .optdb/                       # gitignored: optimization run state
    journal.jsonl               # append-only log of every action+result
    current_run.json            # current optimization state snapshot
    baselines/                  # captured baseline perf numbers
      <func_name>.json
```

**Rationale**: The `opt/` package is importable by Claude Code tools directly. Shell wrappers in `opt/shell/` are thin (5-10 lines) -- they set up PATH and delegate to the existing scripts. The `.optdb/` directory is gitignored for ephemeral state; the `journal.jsonl` provides a crash-recoverable audit trail.

**Q2: Config file format -- TOML**

TOML, not YAML. Reasons:

1. TOML has typed values natively (integers, strings, booleans, arrays) with no implicit coercion. YAML's `on`/`off` -> boolean footgun is particularly dangerous here since the QEMU cfg files use `"on"`/`"off"` strings for ISA features.
2. Python 3.11+ has `tomllib` in stdlib. You already have `tomlkit` (0.13.2) which gives round-trip editing.
3. TOML's `[section]` syntax maps cleanly to the hierarchical config (toolchain, target, qemu, fpga).
4. The QEMU `.cfg` files already use INI-like `[section]` syntax with `property = "value"`, making TOML feel natural for users who edit both.

**Q3: Agent loop -- lightweight state machine, NOT a formal FSM library**

Use a simple loop with an explicit `Phase` enum and a journal. The phases are:

```
ANALYZE -> PLAN -> IMPLEMENT -> BUILD -> VALIDATE_QEMU -> VALIDATE_FPGA -> MEASURE -> EVALUATE -> (loop or DONE)
```

This is NOT a full FSM with transition tables. It is a while loop with a phase variable and a `match` statement. The journal captures every transition. If Claude Code is interrupted, it reads the journal on restart and resumes at the last incomplete phase.

**Rationale**: A formal FSM library (e.g., `transitions`) adds a dependency and abstraction that is harder for Claude Code to reason about. The explicit loop with phases is readable, debuggable, and trivially serializable. Claude Code's agent can also skip phases (e.g., skip `VALIDATE_FPGA` in dry-run mode).

**Q4: Input shapes -- dual approach**

- **Default**: Use the existing test cases in `Examples/unit_func/t_*.c`. The shapes are hardcoded in the `main()` functions. The parser extracts them by reading the C source and parsing the variable assignments in the `#if !ENA_MEASURE_PERF` and `ENA_MEASURE_PERF` blocks. The perf-mode test (test-3 in the conv example) is always present and uses the largest/most representative shape.
- **Custom shapes**: For model mode, shapes are specified in a TOML model definition file. These shapes are injected by generating a custom test harness (a temporary `.c` file) or by using the existing test with different golden data paths. Given the existing test infrastructure relies on pre-computed golden data in `bin/`, the pragmatic approach is: use QEMU in correctness mode with the existing test shapes, but when measuring FPGA cycles for model-relevant shapes, generate a custom test harness that calls the function with the specified shape (without golden-data verification, since the QEMU correctness pass already validates the algorithm).

**Q5: Minimal dependency set**

Python stdlib plus these (all already installed):
- `tomlkit` -- config read/write
- `pydantic` -- config validation and state models
- `click` -- CLI

No new pip installs required. The shell scripts use only `bash`, `expect`, `strace`, and the Andes toolchain binaries.

**Q6: Dry-run mode**

Yes. Dry-run mode means:
- Build: runs normally (uses local toolchain)
- QEMU validation: runs normally (QEMU is local)
- FPGA measurement: **skipped** -- the agent reports "FPGA dry-run: skipped" and uses estimated cycle counts from the objdump instruction count as a rough proxy (or cached baseline values)

This is controlled by a `dry_run = true` flag in `config.toml` and also a `--dry-run` CLI flag.

---

### 2. Component Design

#### 2.1 `config.toml` and `config.py`

The configuration file at `/home/nick/work/agentic_libnn_opt/opt/config.toml`:

```toml
[toolchain]
bin_dir = "/local/nick/SW_Release_cp/ast530/nds64le-elf-newlib-v5d/bin"
prefix = "riscv64-elf-"
compiler = "riscv64-elf-gcc"

[target]
cpu = "AX45MPV"
# SID .conf file -- parsed to extract VLEN, ELEN, ISA extensions
sid_conf = "project/ax45mpv/ADP-AE350-AX45MPV-1C.conf"
# QEMU .cfg file -- parsed to extract QEMU-level features
qemu_cfg = "project/ax45mpv/qemu_cfg/zve64d/ADP-AE350-AX45MPV-1C_dw0_mw1.cfg"

[qemu]
binary = "/local/nick/qemu_andes/build/qemu-system-riscv64"
vlen = 1024
timeout = 120
use_strace = true

[fpga]
host = "sw-boards.andestech.com"
port = 1116
timeout = 300
dry_run = false

[agent]
max_iterations = 10
min_improvement_pct = 2.0  # stop if improvement < 2%
```

`config.py` contains Pydantic models that:
1. Load and validate `config.toml`
2. Parse the QEMU `.cfg` file to auto-extract target features (VLEN, ELEN, zba, zbb, zfh, zvfh, vdot, etc.)
3. Parse the SID `.conf` file to extract `cpu-option` flags (the `--conf-isa-vdot`, `--conf-isa-vpfh`, etc.)
4. Expose a `TargetFeatures` dataclass with all hardware capabilities

The parser for the `.cfg` file is straightforward: it is INI-like with `[global]` sections containing `property`/`value` pairs for the `andes-ax45mpv-riscv-cpu` driver, plus `[andes-config "cpu"]` sections with `key = "value"` pairs.

#### 2.2 `runner.py` -- Command Executor

This module wraps every external command. It is the ONLY module that calls `subprocess`. Every call:
1. Logs the full command to the journal
2. Captures stdout, stderr, return code
3. Returns a `RunResult` dataclass with all three plus wall-clock time

Key methods:
- `build_libnn(cpu: str) -> RunResult` -- calls `build.sh`
- `rebuild_single(source_path: str) -> RunResult` -- calls `rebuild_single.sh` for incremental builds
- `qemu_test(func_name: str, vlen: int) -> RunResult` -- calls `run_qemu_test.sh`
- `fpga_test(func_name: str) -> RunResult` -- calls `run_fpga_test.sh` or `test_perf.sh` single-func mode
- `objdump_function(func_name: str) -> str` -- extracts disassembly for a specific function from `libnn.objdump`

Each method uses `opt/shell/*.sh` wrappers that handle PATH setup and argument translation.

#### 2.3 `parser.py` -- Output Parsers

Parses all structured output from the shell tools:
- `parse_qemu_result(output: str) -> QemuResult` -- extracts PASS/FAIL and sub-test counts from strace output
- `parse_fpga_result(output: str) -> FpgaResult` -- extracts cycle count, instruction count, IPC, pass/fail
- `parse_perf_csv(path: str) -> dict[str, PerfRecord]` -- parses the `performance.csv` from full test suite runs
- `parse_qemu_cfg(path: str) -> TargetFeatures` -- extracts HW features from `.cfg` files
- `parse_sid_conf(path: str) -> dict` -- extracts cpu-option from `.conf` files
- `parse_test_shapes(test_c_path: str) -> list[ShapeSpec]` -- extracts shape parameters from `t_*.c` files via regex on the variable assignments (the `in_tensor_dim_x = 32;` pattern)
- `extract_objdump_function(objdump_path: str, func_name: str) -> str` -- extracts a single function's disassembly

#### 2.4 `state.py` -- Optimization State

```python
class Phase(str, Enum):
    ANALYZE = "analyze"        # read source, extract current impl, get baseline
    PLAN = "plan"              # Claude Code plans the optimization approach
    IMPLEMENT = "implement"    # Claude Code edits the source file
    BUILD = "build"            # compile (incremental or full)
    VALIDATE_QEMU = "validate_qemu"  # correctness via QEMU
    VALIDATE_FPGA = "validate_fpga"  # correctness on FPGA (optional)
    MEASURE = "measure"        # cycle count on FPGA
    EVALUATE = "evaluate"      # compare to baseline, decide next step
    DONE = "done"

class OptimizationState(BaseModel):
    run_id: str
    func_name: str
    source_path: str
    phase: Phase
    iteration: int
    baseline_cycles: int | None
    current_cycles: int | None
    best_cycles: int | None
    best_iteration: int | None
    history: list[IterationRecord]
```

The journal (`journal.jsonl`) is an append-only file where each line is a JSON object:
```json
{"ts": "2026-04-15T10:30:00", "run_id": "abc123", "phase": "build", "action": "rebuild_single", "cmd": "...", "rc": 0, "duration_s": 2.3, "result_summary": "OK"}
```

On crash recovery, `state.py` reads the journal, reconstructs the state, and resumes.

#### 2.5 `shapes.py` -- Shape Specifications

For single-function mode:
```python
@dataclass
class ConvShape:
    in_x: int; in_y: int; in_ch: int; batch: int
    out_ch: int; ker_x: int; ker_y: int
    pad_x: int; pad_y: int; stride_x: int; stride_y: int
```

For model mode, a TOML file like `opt/models/example_yolov8n_s8.toml`:
```toml
name = "YOLOv8n-s8"

[[ops]]
func = "conv_1x1_HWC_s8_s8_s8_asym_bias_fast_any"
shapes = { in_x = 80, in_y = 80, in_ch = 64, batch = 1, out_ch = 64, ker_x = 1, ker_y = 1 }
count = 3  # appears 3 times in the model

[[ops]]
func = "conv_HWC_s8_s8_s8_asym_bias_any"
shapes = { in_x = 40, in_y = 40, in_ch = 128, batch = 1, out_ch = 128, ker_x = 3, ker_y = 3 }
count = 6
```

The `shapes.py` module also provides `extract_shapes_from_test(test_c_path)` that parses existing test C files to pull out the shape parameters. This reuses the `dump_param()` format string as a parsing guide.

#### 2.6 `agent.py` -- The Agent Loop

This is the main entry point for Claude Code. Two modes:

**Single-function mode:**
```
python -m opt run --func conv_HWC_s8_s8_s8_asym_bias_any
```

The agent loop:
```python
def run_optimization(func_name: str, config: Config):
    state = OptimizationState.init(func_name, config)
    
    while state.phase != Phase.DONE and state.iteration < config.agent.max_iterations:
        match state.phase:
            case Phase.ANALYZE:
                # 1. Find source file: Source/*/<func_name>.c
                # 2. Read it, identify current impl variant (plain/DSP/RVV)
                # 3. Get baseline cycles (from cache or FPGA run)
                # 4. Extract objdump for the function
                # 5. Transition -> PLAN
                
            case Phase.PLAN:
                # Claude Code reviews source + objdump + hardware features
                # Produces a plan dict: { "approach": "...", "changes": [...] }
                # Transition -> IMPLEMENT
                
            case Phase.IMPLEMENT:
                # Claude Code edits the source file
                # Exactly ONE logical change per iteration
                # Transition -> BUILD
                
            case Phase.BUILD:
                # Call rebuild_single.sh for the changed file
                # On failure -> revert and go to PLAN with error context
                # On success -> VALIDATE_QEMU
                
            case Phase.VALIDATE_QEMU:
                # Run QEMU test (all VLENs: 1024, 512, 256)
                # On failure -> revert and go to PLAN with error context
                # On success -> MEASURE (or VALIDATE_FPGA if not dry_run)
                
            case Phase.VALIDATE_FPGA:
                # Optional: run on FPGA for correctness
                # Skipped in dry_run mode
                
            case Phase.MEASURE:
                # Run FPGA perf test, get cycle count
                # Skipped in dry_run mode (use objdump inst count estimate)
                
            case Phase.EVALUATE:
                # Compare current_cycles to baseline_cycles
                # If improvement >= threshold -> accept, update best
                # If regression -> revert
                # If max_iterations reached -> DONE
                # Else -> PLAN for next iteration
```

**Model mode:**
```
python -m opt run --model models/yolov8n_s8.toml
```

In model mode, the agent iterates over the operator list sorted by `count * baseline_cycles` (highest total cost first). It runs the single-function optimization loop for each operator, then reports the total model-level improvement.

#### 2.7 `cli.py` -- Click CLI

```
python -m opt run --func <name>          # single function optimization
python -m opt run --model <path.toml>    # model-level optimization
python -m opt run --func <name> --dry-run  # no FPGA
python -m opt status                     # show current optimization state
python -m opt baseline --func <name>     # capture baseline perf numbers
python -m opt baseline --all             # capture all baselines
python -m opt features                   # dump parsed target features
python -m opt journal                    # show journal tail
```

---

### 3. Data Flow

```
User/Claude Code
      |
      v
  cli.py (click)
      |
      v
  agent.py (phase loop)
      |
      +---> config.py (read config.toml, parse .cfg/.conf for features)
      |
      +---> state.py (read/write .optdb/current_run.json + journal.jsonl)
      |
      +---> runner.py (subprocess calls)
      |        |
      |        +---> opt/shell/build.sh -----> ../../build.sh
      |        +---> opt/shell/rebuild_single.sh -> ../../qemu-validate/rebuild_single.sh
      |        +---> opt/shell/qemu_test.sh ----> ../../qemu-validate/run_qemu_test.sh
      |        +---> opt/shell/fpga_test.sh ----> ../../qemu-validate/run_fpga_test.sh
      |        |                                  (or ../../test_perf.sh single-func mode)
      |        v
      |     RunResult (stdout, stderr, rc, duration)
      |
      +---> parser.py (parse RunResult -> structured data)
               |
               v
          QemuResult / FpgaResult / TargetFeatures / ShapeSpec
```

---

### 4. Integration with Claude Code

Claude Code interacts with this system in two ways:

**As an interactive tool user**: Claude Code calls `python -m opt baseline --func <name>` to get baseline numbers, reads source files directly, edits them, then calls `python -m opt run --func <name> --dry-run` to validate.

**As the optimization agent**: The `agent.py` loop is designed so that Claude Code IS the agent. The `PLAN` and `IMPLEMENT` phases are where Claude Code does its work (reading source, analyzing objdump, writing code). The other phases are automated (build, test, measure). The journal gives Claude Code context when resuming.

The key insight is that `agent.py` does NOT need to call Claude Code's API. Instead, Claude Code runs the `agent.py` phases as tools, and the human-in-the-loop (or Claude Code itself in autonomous mode) handles the PLAN and IMPLEMENT phases by reading files and editing code. The agent loop becomes a structured TODO list that Claude Code follows.

For fully autonomous operation, `agent.py` would expose the phase-appropriate context as tool output (e.g., "You are in PLAN phase. Source code: ..., Baseline cycles: ..., Hardware features: ..."), and Claude Code generates the plan + implementation.

---

### 5. Key Implementation Details

**Incremental builds are critical**: A full `build.sh AX45MPV` compiles ~407 files. The `rebuild_single.sh` script compiles 1 file and updates the `.a` archive, making the edit-build-test cycle ~100x faster. The runner should always use incremental builds during optimization and only do a full build at the start (to create `lib_objs/`).

**QEMU validation at multiple VLENs**: The QEMU test should run at VLEN=1024 (primary target) and VLEN=256 (portability check). The QEMU command line already supports `vlen=N` override. Fail at any VLEN = revert.

**FPGA is a shared resource**: The FPGA board at `sw-boards.andestech.com:1116` is a single-threaded resource (GDB connection). The runner must acquire a lock before FPGA operations. A simple file lock (`/tmp/libnn_fpga.lock`) suffices.

**Source file location**: Given function name `conv_HWC_s8_s8_s8_asym_bias_any`, the source file is `Source/ConvolutionFunctions/riscv_nn_conv_HWC_s8_s8_s8_asym_bias_any.c`. The mapping is: `Source/<Category>/riscv_nn_<func_name>.c`. The category can be resolved by globbing `Source/*/riscv_nn_<func_name>.c`.

**Revert mechanism**: Before each IMPLEMENT phase, `runner.py` creates a backup of the source file (e.g., `cp source.c source.c.bak`). On revert, it restores the backup and rebuilds. The git working tree is NOT used for revert (avoids accidental commits).

---

### 6. Implementation Sequence

1. **Phase 1**: `config.toml` + `config.py` + `parser.py` (cfg/conf parsing) -- enables `python -m opt features`
2. **Phase 2**: `runner.py` + `opt/shell/*.sh` wrappers -- enables `python -m opt baseline`
3. **Phase 3**: `state.py` + `agent.py` (single-func mode, QEMU-only dry-run) -- the core loop
4. **Phase 4**: `shapes.py` + model TOML format -- enables model mode
5. **Phase 5**: `cli.py` polish, journal viewer, status dashboard

---

### 7. Potential Challenges

1. **Test shape extraction from C source**: The `t_*.c` files have varied formats. Some use `uint16_t` variables, some use `uint32_t`, some use `int32_t`. The parser needs to handle the `main()` function's variable assignments robustly. Regex on `<var_name> = <value>;` patterns should work for 95% of cases. The remaining 5% can fall back to "use existing test as-is."

2. **FPGA flakiness**: Board connections can drop. The GDB expect script has a 600-second timeout. `runner.py` should retry FPGA operations once on failure, and the journal should log connection errors distinctly from test failures.

3. **Baseline stability**: FPGA cycle counts have some variance (typically <1%). The baseline measurement should run 2-3 times and take the minimum. The `ENA_RUN_TWICE` macro in the existing test infrastructure already helps (the second run is with warm caches).

4. **Multiple implementation variants**: A source file can have up to 3 code paths (`#ifdef ENA_VEC_ISA`, `#ifdef ENA_DSP_ISA`, plain C). The agent needs to know which path is active for the target CPU. The `config.py` module resolves this from the build flags (e.g., AX45MPV always has `ENA_VEC_ISA`).

### Critical Files for Implementation
- `/home/nick/work/agentic_libnn_opt/build.sh` -- must be wrapped, its CPU-to-flags mapping is the source of truth for compilation flags
- `/home/nick/work/agentic_libnn_opt/qemu-validate/run_qemu_test.sh` -- the QEMU validation script to wrap
- `/home/nick/work/agentic_libnn_opt/qemu-validate/rebuild_single.sh` -- the incremental build script to wrap
- `/home/nick/work/agentic_libnn_opt/test_perf.sh` -- the FPGA perf measurement script (single-func mode at line 263-311)
- `/home/nick/work/agentic_libnn_opt/project/ax45mpv/qemu_cfg/zve64d/ADP-AE350-AX45MPV-1C_dw0_mw1.cfg` -- the QEMU config file that the parser needs to extract hardware features from

# Implementation Plan: Autonomous Agentic libnn Optimization Workflow

## Context

libnn functions have multi-variant C implementations (plain C / DSP / RVV). Optimizing a function means writing a better RVV implementation for a specific FPGA target. Currently this is manual: read source, write code, build, test on QEMU, profile on FPGA, iterate.

This plan builds tooling so Claude Code can autonomously execute this optimization loop given a configuration file and a function/model specification. The tooling lives in `opt/` at the repo root.

## Final Directory Structure

```
agentic_libnn_opt/
  roadmap.md                              # High-level vision document
  opt/
    __init__.py
    __main__.py                           # python -m opt entry point
    cli.py                                # Click CLI
    config.py                             # Config schema + loader (Pydantic)
    feature_extractor.py                  # Parse .cfg/.conf -> TargetFeatures
    flag_mapper.py                        # TargetFeatures -> compiler flags
    runner.py                             # Subprocess wrapper (build/test/perf)
    parser.py                             # Parse QEMU/FPGA/objdump output
    task_spec.py                          # Single-func + model task specs
    state.py                              # Optimization state + journal
    workflow.py                           # Agent loop controller
    shell/
      build.sh                            # Thin wrapper around ../../build.sh
      rebuild_single.sh                   # Thin wrapper around ../../qemu-validate/rebuild_single.sh
      qemu_test.sh                        # Thin wrapper around ../../qemu-validate/run_qemu_test.sh
      fpga_perf.sh                        # Thin wrapper around ../../test_perf.sh single-func mode
    models/
      example_yolov8n_s8.toml             # Example model definition
    config.toml                           # Default user config
  tests/
    __init__.py
    conftest.py                           # Shared fixtures (paths, sample configs)
    test_config.py
    test_feature_extractor.py
    test_flag_mapper.py
    test_runner.py
    test_parser.py
    test_task_spec.py
    test_state.py
    test_workflow.py
  .optstate/                              # gitignored: runtime state
    journal.jsonl
    baselines/
```

## Dependencies

- Python 3.11+ (tomllib in stdlib)
- `pydantic` - config/state validation
- `click` - CLI
- `tomlkit` - TOML read/write (already installed)
- No other new dependencies

---

## Step 1: Project Scaffold and roadmap.md

**Feature name:** `project-scaffold`

**Description:** Create the `opt/` package directory structure, `tests/` directory, `.optstate/` gitignore entry, `pyproject.toml` for the package, and `roadmap.md` describing the project vision.

**Key files to create/modify:**
- `roadmap.md` - high-level vision document
- `opt/__init__.py` - empty package init
- `opt/__main__.py` - stub: `print("opt: not yet implemented")`
- `tests/__init__.py` - empty
- `tests/conftest.py` - shared fixtures (repo root path, sample config path)
- `pyproject.toml` - package metadata, dependencies (pydantic, click, tomlkit)
- `.gitignore` - add `.optstate/` entry

**Dependencies:** None

**Test cases:**
1. `python -m opt` prints stub message and exits 0
2. `python -m pytest tests/ --collect-only` discovers test directory (0 tests, no errors)
3. `roadmap.md` exists and is non-empty

**Verification:**
```bash
python -m opt
python -m pytest tests/ --collect-only
test -f roadmap.md
```

---

## Step 2: Configuration Schema and Loader

**Feature name:** `config-schema`

**Description:** Define the TOML configuration file format and Pydantic models for validation. The config specifies toolchain path, target CPU, QEMU path, and FPGA address. Loader reads `opt/config.toml`, validates all fields, and returns a typed `Config` object.

**Key files to create/modify:**
- `opt/config.py` - Pydantic models: `ToolchainConfig`, `TargetConfig`, `QemuConfig`, `FpgaConfig`, `AgentConfig`, top-level `Config`
- `opt/config.toml` - default config populated with current AX45MPV values
- `tests/test_config.py` - unit tests

**Dependencies:** Step 1

**Config format:**
```toml
[toolchain]
bin_dir = "/local/nick/SW_Release_cp/ast530/nds64le-elf-newlib-v5d/bin"
prefix = "riscv64-elf-"
compiler = "gcc"

[target]
cpu = "AX45MPV"
qemu_cfg = "project/ax45mpv/qemu_cfg/zve64d/ADP-AE350-AX45MPV-1C_dw0_mw1.cfg"

[qemu]
binary = "/local/nick/qemu_andes/build/qemu-system-riscv64"
vlen = 1024
timeout = 120
use_strace = true

[fpga]
host = "sw-boards.andestech.com"
port = 1116
timeout = 300

[agent]
max_iterations = 10
min_improvement_pct = 2.0
dry_run = false
```

**Test cases:**
1. Load valid `config.toml` -> all fields populated with correct types
2. Load config with missing `[toolchain]` section -> `ValidationError` with clear message
3. Load config with invalid `cpu = "INVALID_CPU"` -> error listing valid CPU names
4. Load config with missing optional `[agent]` section -> defaults applied (`max_iterations=10`, `dry_run=false`)
5. `Config.repo_root` returns absolute path to the repo root

**Verification:**
```bash
python -m pytest tests/test_config.py -v
```

---

## Step 3: FPGA/QEMU Config Feature Extractor

**Feature name:** `feature-extractor`

**Description:** Parse QEMU `.cfg` files (INI-style with `[global]` sections) to extract CPU hardware features: VLEN, ELEN, ISA extensions, dot product support, etc. Returns a `TargetFeatures` dataclass.

**Key files to create/modify:**
- `opt/feature_extractor.py` - parser for QEMU `.cfg` format, `TargetFeatures` dataclass
- `tests/test_feature_extractor.py` - unit tests using real `.cfg` files in the repo

**Dependencies:** Step 2 (uses `Config.target.qemu_cfg` path)

**Extracted fields:**
- `vlen: int` (from `property = "vlen"`)
- `elen: int` (from `property = "elen"`)
- `cpu_model: str` (from `driver = "andes-ax45mpv-riscv-cpu"`)
- `extensions: set[str]` (zba, zbb, zbc, zbs, zfh, zvfh, zfbfmin, zvfbfmin, zvfbfwma)
- `isa_v: bool` (from `[andes-config "cpu"]` `isa-v`)
- `isa_vdot: bool` (from `isa-vdot`)
- `isa_zvlsseg: bool`
- `isa_vl4: bool`
- `mvec_cfg_dw: int`, `mvec_cfg_mw: int`

**Test cases:**
1. Parse `project/ax45mpv/qemu_cfg/zve64d/ADP-AE350-AX45MPV-1C_dw0_mw1.cfg` -> `vlen=512, elen=64, isa_vdot=True, zfh=True, zvfh=True`
2. Parse `project/ax45mpv/qemu_cfg/zve32x/ADP-AE350-AX45MPV-1C_dw0.cfg` -> `elen=32`, no float extensions
3. Parse non-existent file -> `FileNotFoundError` with path in message
4. Parse file with missing `vlen` property -> error "vlen not found in config"
5. `TargetFeatures.has_vector` property returns True when `isa_v=True`

**Verification:**
```bash
python -m pytest tests/test_feature_extractor.py -v
```

---

## Step 4: Feature-to-Compiler-Flags Mapper

**Feature name:** `flag-mapper`

**Description:** Convert `TargetFeatures` to the exact compiler flags that `build.sh` uses. This is the bridge between hardware config and build system. The mapping must match `build.sh` line-for-line for each CPU variant.

**Key files to create/modify:**
- `opt/flag_mapper.py` - `map_features_to_flags(cpu: str, features: TargetFeatures) -> list[str]`
- `tests/test_flag_mapper.py`

**Dependencies:** Step 3 (consumes `TargetFeatures`)

**Mapping rules (from `build.sh` lines 82-97 for AX45MPV):**
- Base: `-O3 -ffunction-sections -fdata-sections -Wall -Werror`
- `cpu contains "45"` -> `-mtune=andes-45-series -fno-strict-aliasing`
- `isa_v=True` -> `-mext-vector -DENA_VEC_ISA -fno-tree-slp-vectorize -fno-tree-vectorize`
- `vlen` -> `-DMAX_VLEN=<vlen>` (use config override or extracted value)
- `isa_vdot=True` -> `-DENA_NDS_V5_VEC_DOT_PROD`
- RVV version from `mvec_cfg_major` -> `-DNDS_VEC_RVV_VERSION=1000` (major=1 -> 1000)

**Test cases:**
1. AX45MPV features -> flags contain `-DENA_VEC_ISA -DMAX_VLEN=1024 -DNDS_VEC_RVV_VERSION=1000 -DENA_NDS_V5_VEC_DOT_PROD`
2. Non-vector target -> flags do NOT contain `-DENA_VEC_ISA`
3. Flags include base optimization flags (`-O3 -Wall -Werror`)
4. Integration: build with generated flags, compare `libnn.objdump` symbol list to build.sh-generated one (identical)

**Verification:**
```bash
python -m pytest tests/test_flag_mapper.py -v
```

---

## Step 5: Shell Wrappers

**Feature name:** `shell-wrappers`

**Description:** Thin shell scripts in `opt/shell/` that set up PATH from config and delegate to existing scripts. Each wrapper reads environment variables set by the Python runner.

**Key files to create:**
- `opt/shell/build.sh` - sets PATH, calls `../../build.sh $CPU "$EXTRA_FLAGS" $COMPILER`
- `opt/shell/rebuild_single.sh` - sets PATH, calls `../../qemu-validate/rebuild_single.sh $SOURCE_FILE`
- `opt/shell/qemu_test.sh` - sets PATH + QEMU vars, calls `../../qemu-validate/run_qemu_test.sh $FUNC --strace --vlen $VLEN`
- `opt/shell/fpga_perf.sh` - sets MYPC/MYPORT, calls `../../test_perf.sh ax45mpv $COMPILER BOARD $FUNC`

**Dependencies:** Step 2 (reads config for paths)

**Test cases:**
1. `opt/shell/build.sh` with valid config env vars -> builds `libnn.a` at repo root
2. `opt/shell/qemu_test.sh` with `FUNC=relu_s8` -> exits 0, output contains "pass"
3. All shell scripts are executable (`chmod +x`)
4. Each script fails cleanly if required env vars are unset

**Verification:**
```bash
# After setting env vars from config:
OPT_TOOLCHAIN_BIN=/local/nick/SW_Release_cp/ast530/nds64le-elf-newlib-v5d/bin \
  bash opt/shell/build.sh AX45MPV
```

---

## Step 6: Command Runner

**Feature name:** `command-runner`

**Description:** Python module that invokes shell wrappers via `subprocess`, captures output, and returns structured `RunResult` objects. Handles timeouts, logging, and error classification. This is the ONLY module that calls `subprocess`.

**Key files to create/modify:**
- `opt/runner.py` - `Runner` class with methods: `build()`, `rebuild_single()`, `qemu_test()`, `fpga_perf()`, `objdump_function()`
- `tests/test_runner.py`

**Dependencies:** Step 2 (Config), Step 5 (shell wrappers)

**Interface:**
```python
@dataclass
class RunResult:
    command: str
    returncode: int
    stdout: str
    stderr: str
    duration_s: float

class Runner:
    def __init__(self, config: Config): ...
    def build(self) -> RunResult: ...
    def rebuild_single(self, source_path: str) -> RunResult: ...
    def qemu_test(self, func_name: str, vlen: int = 1024) -> RunResult: ...
    def fpga_perf(self, func_name: str) -> RunResult: ...
    def objdump_function(self, func_name: str) -> str | None: ...
```

**Test cases:**
1. `Runner.build()` -> `RunResult.returncode == 0`, `libnn.a` exists
2. `Runner.qemu_test("relu_s8")` -> `RunResult.returncode == 0`, stdout contains "pass"
3. `Runner.qemu_test("nonexistent_func")` -> `RunResult.returncode != 0`
4. `Runner.objdump_function("riscv_nn_relu_s8")` -> non-empty string containing vector instructions
5. All `RunResult` objects have `duration_s > 0`
6. Unit tests with mocked subprocess for fast execution

**Verification:**
```bash
python -m pytest tests/test_runner.py -v
```

---

## Step 7: Output Parsers

**Feature name:** `output-parsers`

**Description:** Parse structured output from QEMU tests, FPGA perf tests, and objdump. Convert raw text to typed dataclasses.

**Key files to create/modify:**
- `opt/parser.py` - parse functions for each output type
- `tests/test_parser.py`

**Dependencies:** Step 6 (consumes `RunResult.stdout`)

**Parsers:**
```python
@dataclass
class QemuResult:
    func_name: str
    passed: bool
    sub_tests: int
    sub_passes: int
    output: str

@dataclass
class FpgaResult:
    func_name: str
    cycles: int
    instructions: int
    passed: bool

def parse_qemu_output(stdout: str, func_name: str) -> QemuResult: ...
def parse_fpga_output(stdout: str, func_name: str) -> FpgaResult: ...
def parse_perf_csv(csv_path: str) -> dict[str, FpgaResult]: ...
```

**Test cases:**
1. Parse real QEMU strace output (captured sample) -> correct pass/fail
2. Parse QEMU output with "accuracy checking fail" -> `passed=False`
3. Parse FPGA output with `inst` and `cycle` lines -> positive integer values
4. Parse `performance.csv` from baseline -> dict with function names as keys
5. Parse empty/malformed output -> clear error, not crash

**Verification:**

```bash
python -m pytest tests/test_parser.py -v
```

## Step 8: Single-Function Task Specification

**Feature name:** `task-spec-single`

**Description:** TOML format for specifying a single function optimization task with input shapes. Parser validates function exists in the source tree.

**Key files to create/modify:**
- `opt/task_spec.py` - `SingleFuncTask` Pydantic model, loader
- `tests/test_task_spec.py`

**Dependencies:** Step 2 (Config for repo root path)

**Format:**
```toml

function = "conv_HWC_s8_s8_s8_sym_bias_fast"
optimization_target = "cycles"  # "cycles" or "instructions"

[input_shapes]

in_channels = 64
out_channels = 128

kernel_w = 3
```

**Test cases:**

2. `function` validated: `Source/*/riscv_nn_<function>.c` exists

```bash
python -m pytest tests/test_task_spec.py -v
```

---

## Step 9: Model Task Specification

**Feature name:** `task-spec-model`

**Description:** TOML format for specifying a model as a list of libnn function calls with shapes and call counts. Used for model-level optimization prioritization.

**Key files to create/modify:**
- `opt/task_spec.py` - add `ModelTask` Pydantic model, `OpEntry` model
- `opt/models/example_yolov8n_s8.toml` - example model definition
- `tests/test_task_spec.py` - add model tests

**Dependencies:** Step 8 (extends task_spec.py)

**Format:**
```toml
mode = "model"
name = "YOLOv8n-s8"
optimization_target = "total_cycles"
budget_iterations = 20

[[ops]]
func = "conv_1x1_HWC_s8_s8_s8_asym_bias_fast_any"
call_count = 3
[ops.shapes]
batch = 1
height = 80
width = 80

[[ops]]
```

**Test cases:**

2. All function names validated against source tree
3. `total_weighted_calls` property = sum of call_counts
4. `unique_functions` property returns deduplicated function list
5. Parse model with 0 ops -> error "model must have at least one op"
